differences.title: 1. INITIAL RENDER
differences.leftDesc: HTML sent directly from server, no JS needed for initial render.
differences.rightDesc: Initial render requires JavaScript to download and execute in the browser.

differences.title: 2. DATA FETCHING
differences.leftDesc: Can directly access backend resources and databases without extra network hops.
differences.rightDesc: Relies on network requests (APIs) to fetch data from the server.

differences.title: 3. BUNDLE SIZE
differences.leftDesc: Reduces client-side JavaScript bundle size, improving initial load performance.
differences.rightDesc: Can increase client-side bundle size as components and their logic ship to the browser.

differences.title: 4. INTERACTIVITY
differences.leftDesc: Not directly interactive; requires Client Components for user interactions.
differences.rightDesc: Full control over user interactions, state management, and effects within the browser.

differences.title: 5. SEO
differences.leftDesc: Optimal for Search Engine Optimization as content is readily available in HTML.
differences.rightDesc: SEO is generally good but can be impacted by JavaScript execution delays.

differences.title: 6. DEPENDENCIES
differences.leftDesc: Can use Node.js APIs and libraries not available in the browser.
differences.rightDesc: Must only use browser-compatible APIs and libraries to avoid runtime errors.

differences.title: 7. CACHING
differences.leftDesc: Leverages server-side caching strategies for faster subsequent requests.
differences.rightDesc: Relies on browser caching mechanisms, which can be less predictable.
